chapters.title: A gift of fire or a box of trouble
chapters.text:
Have you ever wondered what life will look like twenty years from now? Will you have more time for family and friends while robots take care of the chores? Or will you lose your job because your bosses think artificial intelligence is better than you? One thing is certain: technology will shape everyone's future.

Some experts see cutting edge tools as a gift of fire that helps humanity solve hard problems. Others compare technology to Pandora's box, a force humanity cannot control. Both futures are possible. The difference comes down to how these inventions are used.

Quantum computers are a good example. If they work as promised, they could transform climate change research and healthcare. But scammers could also use them to crack the codes behind roughly 120 billion dollars worth of Bitcoin. If yours sit in P2PK format, now is a good time to lock them down.

Knowing how AI works helps protect you from manipulation and scams.

Step into a quick trip to 2041 to explore the possible upsides of technology and the price we may have to pay. Let us get going.

chapters.title: Machines and people learn in different ways
chapters.text:
For decades, AI lived inside a small circle of researchers. Why? The idea looked good on paper, but tests were impossible without enough data and enough computing power. Those limits disappeared once the internet made deep learning practical, and the field changed overnight.

What makes deep learning so different? Instead of feeding machines written rules, it lets AI teach itself. People only need to supply many examples and the right answers. Imagine teaching AI to spot dogs in photos. You feed in thousands of pictures labeled dog and thousands labeled no dog, and the machine figures out what makes a dog a dog.

Computing power is the engine of AI, and data is the fuel.

Deep learning sits at the heart of most modern advances, training AI to handle specific tasks. We already feel it in personalized Amazon searches and in the curated feeds on Facebook. In the future, AI could design a diet based on your medical history or build a school program tailored to a child's interests. These tools will take time, because today's deep learning has three big limits. It needs huge amounts of data. It works in one domain. And it has to be aimed at a specific goal.

The easiest wins for deep learning are fields with clear metrics and plenty of data, such as trading, social media, and insurance. The catch is that what entrepreneurs and investors want is not always what serves the public.

For example, a goal like maximize the time users spend on social media can lead AI to manipulate people. Can we set goals that balance company profits with user wellbeing? First, we need to design those goals. Second, we have to convince companies to chase goals more complex than short term profit. Will they agree?

Deep learning also brings other risks.

Prejudice is one. If AI is trained on biased data or misses information about certain groups, the bias gets baked in. That matters in fields like criminal justice.

Lack of explanation is another. AI justifies its choices with math, not reasoning, so it is harder to check whether it made the right call.

AI development has to involve many stakeholders so that new tools serve the public, not only corporate goals.

chapters.title: Can we trust what we see in 2041
chapters.text:
Computer vision sounds technical, but you bump into it all the time. It is what lets phones unlock with a selfie and lets Xbox dance games read your moves. In the future, your phone could read the street around you and suggest the safest route.

There are still hurdles to clear.

Camouflage can fool AI.

Attacks can hijack or steer AI's behavior.

The most famous risk is deepfakes, which rely on generative adversarial networks, or GANs. These networks work in pairs. One network fabricates images of cats and passes them to a second network. The second network compares them against real cat photos and decides whether each image is real or fake. Both networks learn from each round and try to outdo the other.

In theory, this loop settles into a balance. In practice, people with more computing power can create deepfakes that fool systems trained on weaker machines.

There is also a cost problem. Checking ten photos a day is easy, but social media sees thousands of uploads every minute. Kai-Fu Lee predicts that solid deepfake security will mostly be limited to government websites. Other platforms will likely spot check only the viral content to slow the spread of misinformation.

Content authentication offers a ray of hope. The idea is that every photo or video you take includes capture data saved through blockchain, so others cannot quietly alter the file. This could shield us from deepfakes, but only if every recording device adopts the standard, which will take more than twenty years.

In the meantime, here is what we can do.

Improve deepfake detectors.

Pass new laws against fakes.

Use critical thinking when you scroll.

Enjoy the positive uses of GAN, from helping discover new medicines to creating animations based on classic art.

Be careful when posting photos and videos online, since you never know how someone else will use them.

chapters.title: From chatbots to the idea of singularity
chapters.text:
Another fast moving area of AI is natural language processing, or NLP. NLP lets software turn handwriting or speech into text, and powers chatbots that help users with everyday questions. In the future, NLP could grow into AI mentors for children, helping with time management, walking them through tough tasks, and teaching subjects in a personal way.

Where do things stand today? For twenty years, NLP leaned on a huge industry of data labeling. For example, a customer might say the oven is defective or I do not want to use your service anymore, and both phrases mean the same thing: refund please. Specialists had to label these sentences so machines could read the customer's intent.

A recent breakthrough called sequence transduction has changed the game. Now machines can pick up the meaning of words from the surrounding context. GPT-3 is one of the largest and best known sequence transduction models. That is why context matters so much when you work with it. For example, you might write, An elephant is heavier than a cow. A book is heavier than a pencil. What is heavier, a trolleybus or a cable car? The first two lines set the meaning of heavier and keep the model from getting confused.

Even with that help, GPT-3 has limits.

It does not realize what it does not know. When it cannot find an answer in its data, it starts hallucinating and inventing plausible sounding output.

GPT-3 learns from massive amounts of text, including biased and hateful content, and that bias can show up in its answers.

Despite these flaws, GPT models deliver stunning results that improve with each new release. Some experts believe these models will one day match or surpass the human brain. The latter idea is known as singularity, and views differ. Utopians think it will give everyone a comfortable life. Dystopians think super intelligent AI will threaten humanity.

Machines win on quantity, so humans should lead on depth and perspective.

Kai-Fu Lee thinks it is too early to talk about singularity. AI still lacks a few core abilities.

Counterfactual thinking.

Reasoning.

Strategic thinking.

Each one needs a breakthrough, and breakthroughs are rare. The last big one, deep learning, arrived over sixty years ago.

One striking detail. To read every word GPT-3 was trained on, a person would need about 500,000 lifetimes.

chapters.title: Trading privacy for comfort
chapters.text:
Devices already know a lot about us, perhaps too much. And that is just the start. As AI improves, it needs more data, so privacy issues will keep popping up across every tech field.

Healthcare shows the pattern. Our medical records are going digital, alongside data on treatments and clinical trials. In the future, the Internet of Things will add another layer. Smart toothbrushes, smart beds, and other gadgets will collect health data, giving AI real cases to learn from.

Over time, AI will become a key partner for doctors in fields like radiology, diagnostic eye care, and pathology. Some experts think AI will be good enough to diagnose and advise patients, leaving human doctors to focus on the empathy and explanation that machines cannot offer. This shift opens the door to precision medicine, where treatments match the patient instead of one size fits all advice.

To get there, people have to share their data, which raises hard questions.

Will people share their data, and on what terms? Picture companies offering discounts for personal data. Would that create a healthcare gap, where AI serves poorer patients while premium human care is reserved for the rich?

Could the control of data turn some companies into monopolies?

How should governments regulate this area?

The European Union has already acted by introducing the General Data Protection Regulation. It requires companies to explain why they collect data and sets heavy fines for breaking the rules.

Kai-Fu Lee calls some of those rules unrealistic and worries they will slow AI progress. He suggests letting a trusted AI decide which services should receive our data. Candidates to run that trusted AI include a benevolent monarchy, a digital commune, or a non profit. The question is whether any of them would care about us more than we care about ourselves. Would we just be handing control of our lives to someone else?

Take a moment to check the permissions on your phone and turn off the ones you do not need.

chapters.title: Which robots can safely share space with people
chapters.text:
From large factory machines to small robotic vacuums at home, robots are quietly working their way into daily life. Soon they will be common in agriculture, raising yields and cutting costs. Warehouses, hotels, complex surgeries, and round the clock labs are other promising fields. Some inventions, however, need more time. Autonomous vehicles, or AVs, could slash transportation costs, but they are not ready for prime time.

Driving covers endless scenarios, from weather and traffic to road quality and vehicle type. Collecting data on every possible case is impossible, so researchers have suggested synthesizing data. Training AI on probable but not real scenarios could raise the error rate, and the cost of mistakes on the road is high. There are also deeper questions to answer, like what AI should do when an accident is unavoidable and who is responsible for the harm caused.

AV companies tend to fall into two camps.

Some test for years before launching the safest possible vehicles, like Waymo.

Others sell relatively safe cars and gather data from real world outcomes, like Tesla.

Before you back any tech company, check their ethical principles.

Researchers are also exploring other approaches. What if cities redesigned roads to separate AVs from pedestrians, removing the chance of crashes? Sensors in the road could add another layer of safety.

Even with these measures, there are edge cases like hacking attempts or natural disasters. One idea is tele operations centers, where trained human drivers take over from AI drivers during emergencies. They would receive a stream of data from around the car and, with augmented reality and 6G, virtually drive the vehicle through the crisis.

Should we ever let a machine make choices that could harm human lives? If the answer is no, autonomous vehicles would never leave the test track.

Autonomous vehicles have fans and critics. Autonomous weapons, on the other hand, are almost certain to destroy humanity. They would make it cheap and easy to commit crimes and even start wars. Since AI does not think about the long term results of its choices, it could even trigger a nuclear conflict.

There is only one answer for autonomous weapons. Ban them. Every country agrees in principle, except Russia, the United Kingdom, and the United States.

chapters.title: Will everyone lose their job
chapters.text:
According to Kai-Fu Lee, machines will replace human workers in over a third of all professions by the early 2030s. Looking at AI's strengths and weaknesses helps us see which jobs are most at risk.

AI handles repetitive tasks and pattern recognition well. Workers with routine duties, like warehouse pickers, face a high chance of being replaced. Loan underwriting, hiring, and other jobs that lean on analytical skills are also easy targets for AI.

The list of vulnerable jobs will keep growing. As more people work remotely, data about their tasks and communication goes digital. That gives AI the chance to learn new skills quickly.

Some skills are out of reach for machines.

Inventiveness. AI cannot dream up new ideas, blend concepts from different fields, or plan long term strategies.

Dexterity. Delicate physical work that requires precise hand eye coordination is beyond machines.

Empathy. Technology cannot genuinely care for others or grasp the full depth of human experience.

So which jobs should we aim for?

Creative and social. Executive roles that mix strategy and team management depend on human skills.

Routine but social. Schools in 2041 will lean on AI for repetitive tasks like grading, while teachers focus on mentoring.

Creative and asocial. Researchers will design experiments and review results while robots handle twenty four seven lab work.

Social and physical. Caregiving is out of reach for robots.

New AI related roles. The surge in technology will create fresh jobs like robot mechanics and AI engineers.

Build your creative and social skills, since they are your edge over machines.

Even though many fields still need a human touch, plenty of people will lose their jobs. Without guardrails, automation could push society into a new shape where most citizens have little power and a few tech leaders hold most of the influence. How do we steer clear of that outcome?

Retrain workers in vulnerable fields for free.

Roll out universal basic income so everyone has their basic needs met.

UBI has two challenges. First, a stipend does not replace the purpose and pride that come from work. Second, governments plan to fund UBI by taxing tech giants, yet some of those magnates already dodge taxes. Will they suddenly pay up when their income multiplies?

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Technology can be a companion that makes life easier or a tool that controls us. Much depends on the people who build these machines, but our own attitudes matter just as much. As customers, clients, and voters, we have the power to push AI in the right direction. The first step is understanding the technology. In the past, that kind of knowledge might have earned you the label of geek. Today, it makes you a responsible citizen.

What does it look like to stay informed about tech news?

Research the upsides and downsides of a new tool before you use it.

Look into the ethics of the companies behind the technology.

Think critically and ask questions whenever machines fail, make mistakes, or get hacked.

Together we can shape a promising future for everyone.

A few things to try. Read the terms before clicking Accept on a new app. Be careful when sharing content online so you do not spread misinformation. Build a daily habit of learning more about technology.
